title: Understanding the Problem
text: To find out how long the kangaroo is in the air, we need to consider the motion of the kangaroo as it jumps over the object. This involves understanding the vertical motion and using the appropriate kinematic equations.

title: Identify the Known Values
text: The kangaroo jumps over an object that is $$2.50$$ $$m$$ high. This height is the maximum height reached by the kangaroo during its jump.

title: Using Kinematic Equations
text: The kinematic equation for vertical motion is: $$v^2=u^2+2a s$$, where v is the final velocity, u is the initial velocity, a is the acceleration due to gravity, and s is the displacement (height in this case).

title: Solving for Initial Velocity
text: At the maximum height, the final velocity (v) is $$0$$ $$\frac{m}{s}$$. Use the equation $$0=u^2+2\left(-9.8\right)\times2.5$$ to solve for the initial velocity (u).

title: Calculate Initial Velocity
text: Calculate the initial velocity using the equation: $$0=u^2+2\left(-9.8\right)\times2.5$$.

title: Time to Reach Maximum Height
text: Use the equation $$v=u+a t$$ to find the time to reach the maximum height. Here, $$v=0$$, $$u=7$$, and $$a=-9.8$$.

title: Calculate Time to Maximum Height
text: Calculate the time to reach maximum height using the equation: $$0=7+\left(-9.8\right) t$$.

title: Total Time in the Air
text: The total time in the air is twice the time to reach the maximum height, as the time to ascend and descend are equal.

title: Calculate Total Time in the Air
text: Multiply the time to reach maximum height by $$2$$ to find the total time in the air.
